title: Open source is dying
rawText: Open source is incredibly important to me. I can say confidently I would not be here today if it wasn't for open- source software. It's a huge part of how I started my career, got into YouTube, and made all of this happen. Life without open source is genuinely hard for me to imagine, which is why I'm really scared right now. We're finally at the point where AI is having a real impact on open source. And if I'm being frank, it's not very good. And no, I'm not just talking about the people on Reddit who are mad because GitHub isn't for them and they can't find the download button. As funny as these posts are, I'm talking more about stuff like this Tail Draw's announcement that they're automatically closing PRs from external contributors going forward. Increased requirements for reporting bugs in projects like Node.js where you can't just flag it anymore cuz they're getting so much AI spam. Or even the Tailwind drama I covered earlier this year that has been massively impacting their ability to fund the team and project. This isn't good. If things keep going in this direction, not only will it destroy open source, it might destroy software as a whole. Having these core pieces we can all rely on together as an industry is essential for our success building software. And it does not matter how good AI is. If it takes this from us, we are doomed. And that means we ought to fight a little bit. It's more important now than ever that we as experienced developers put our foot down and do everything we can to prevent this spiral from killing open source. There's a lot of different problems in open source right now, and one of the biggest is funding. Thankfully, sponsors have helped a lot, just like today's sponsor. Y'all might have seen a new project I recently launched, T3 Code. It's the best way to code with AI. But we have a couple problems. Specifically, this number. The number of pull requests we're getting is insane. And the amount of CI we'd have to run and wait for there would be even more insane. If we turn it on by default for all users, we have a huge potential security risk. But if we leave it off by default and go click the button when we want to run an action, we're going to be stuck there waiting forever before we even know if the code works. Well, that was the case before we started using today's sponsor, Blacksmith. The best way to run your GitHub actions. You make a oneline change, and yes, it is literally a oneline change, and now your actions are twice as fast and cost half as much. Yes, that does add up to a 75% savings in most cases. Don't believe me? You can go look at the PR yourself. This PR that I didn't even file, I literally just clicked the onboard button in the Blacksmith dashboard and it filed this PR for me. I thought it might be more complex because this codebase is building Electron across different things and whatnot. Nope. Literally just changed from Abuntu latest to Blacksmith for CPU. That was it. Everything worked. Not only did everything work, it worked way faster. Our CI times for this app got cut in over half from about 2 and 1/2 minutes to under a minute consistently. What's even better is their dashboard, though. We had a couple tests that were a little bit flaky. And the dashboard has made it so easy to figure out which tests are failing, which ones are succeeding, and why. Seriously though, how cool is this? Having actual metrics of what things are succeeding and failing and being able to see like, oh, we had a huge spike in failures at this time yesterday. So useful to figure out what's going right and wrong in your work. And I can click here and see over time where the failures and successes were happening. And I could even go through and monitor, look at specific events. It is suddenly so much easier to figure out what's going wrong when things are failing. Stop wasting your time and money and sign up today at soy.link/blacksmith. There's a couple different angles I want to take this one down. Specifically, we're going to start with the PR spam and slot problem. But then there's the dumber, angrier users problem, install versus prompt, and biggest probably is the funding problems. First, we're going to start with the PR spam problem. I've been pretty aware of this for a while, and I have a lot of sympathy for people like TL Draw that put out this change where they're just going to start closing any external contributions because they're just getting flooded with them. And I knew how bad this was and it helped other maintainers dealing with it in the past. But this problem is much closer to my heart now because we're going through it ourselves. The T3 codebase, which has been public for about 5 days now, has 150 open PRs. Mind you, we specified that we're not accepting contributions and we still got 100 PRs a day for the first two days. I eventually added this cuz I was tired of saying the same things over and over again in all of the PRs. We're not actively accepting PRs. You can still open issue PR, but please do so knowing there's a high chance we close it, defer it forever, and never look at it. If that sounds annoying, it's because it is. Project's still very early. We're trying to keep scope, quality, and direction under control. Yeah, there's a very real problem with software development around what I don't really know how to explain other than understanding of the system. When you build a project yourself, you understand the whole system. When you build it with AI, you might understand how the parts come together, but your grasp over the whole system is inherently going to go down the more and more you defer to the AI to do things. When you start accepting PRs, similar stuff happens. When you start accepting a lot of PRs from external sources, it gets worse. And then when those are built with AI, it just the the slop expands aggressively. If you understand 100% of your codebase and then you merge a change that you don't understand 5% of and then that happens again and again and again, you very quickly end up in a position where you don't actually understand very much of the codebase at all. And it becomes way harder to maintain control as that happens. Previously, the only way you'd get in this position is by accepting too many PRs. Now, you can do it yourself by coding with agents. And you can do it exponentially worse by coding with agents and merging PRs from other people with agents. And that's assuming you can get through them all because there's 150 PRs. It's kind of intense. Like, this is a lot to deal with. One of the things we're trying out is vouch. We'll definitely talk about that when we get more to solutions, but I really wanted to emphasize how bad this is right now. There is no world in which we can give a proper review to every single PR we are getting at this rate with just Julius and me doing all of these reviews. I basically couldn't do anything Monday cuz I was so tired from the whole weekend of triaging, managing PRs, going through issues. I had to bring two people on just to help keep track of the PRs and issues. It's insane. It's so much more work than I would have imagined. And I already was imagining it to be a lot of work. Now, I'm going to ask an interesting question. I actually don't know what you guys will answer here. Do you get more or less tech questions since AI started to blow up? I am very curious where this lands for y'all. Now that the qu's been up for a little bit, make sure you pick your decision before I say what I'm about to say. I personally find I'm getting way less, which has been interesting. I get significantly fewer of these types of questions than I used to. It feels like the questions I've been getting are different now where I'm suddenly getting like random Twitch streamers hitting me up about their vibecoded chat app for not chatting with AI but having their chat shown in their stream or I'm talking to people who I used to work with at Twitch that were more on the product side that are building their own solutions to random video problems or I'm talking to people who I know from like high school that are building apps for their friends restaurants and [ __ ] and none of them knew how to code and all of them are confused and the AI gets them pretty far. But what ends up happening is I get these weird questions that are full of technical terms, a handful of which are even being used correctly, but the question is like missing from it somehow. It's hard to describe, but it's this uncanny thing where even if the question is simple, the amount of mental energy it takes for me to parse the question out of it has gone up a ton. It's weird. It leans It's similar to [ __ ] bench. I get questions that feel like [ __ ] bench. I feel like that goes deeper than it might seem initially. And I know I've experienced this as an open- source maintainer and developer myself. I have had a lot of people who are really confused that their T3 chat sub doesn't work in T3 code.
